skill.name: aws-security-audit
skill.description: Assess AWS security posture with guided AWS CLI and boto3 checks for IAM, network, logging, and data protection gaps.
skill.summary: Run practical AWS security posture checks with CLI-focused guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk strings, but review found they are mostly AWS security-audit examples, public CIDR checks, and encryption terminology. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, destructive AWS action, or malicious network endpoint was found. The skill remains medium risk because it encourages local shell execution and AWS account enumeration using active credentials.

medium_findings.title: Local AWS CLI Commands Enumerate Cloud Resources
medium_findings.description: The skill provides shell commands that query IAM users, access keys, security groups, S3 buckets, RDS instances, EBS volumes, KMS keys, CloudTrail, and AWS Config. This is legitimate for an AWS audit, but users must run it only with authorized, least-privilege credentials because outputs can reveal sensitive account inventory and security posture.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are real AWS CLI calls that enumerate account resources. The surrounding context is a legitimate audit guide, so the risk is operational exposure rather than malicious execution.

medium_findings.title: Copyable Audit Script Runs Multiple External Commands
medium_findings.description: The automated audit script combines several AWS CLI commands into a copyable bash script. It appears read-only and does not send data to third-party endpoints, but it can produce sensitive findings and depends on the caller's AWS permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script is directly executable and invokes external AWS commands. Review found no destructive action or exfiltration target, which keeps the severity moderate.

medium_findings.title: Boto3 Example Uses Live AWS API Clients
medium_findings.description: The Python scoring example creates boto3 clients and queries IAM, EC2, and S3 data. This is appropriate for the stated use case, but it requires AWS credentials and can expose details about cloud controls in generated output.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly initializes AWS SDK clients and queries account resources. It does not include suspicious endpoints or data transfer outside AWS.

low_findings.title: Public CIDR and Documentation URLs Are Audit References
low_findings.description: Hardcoded 0.0.0.0/0 values are used as findings to detect open security groups, not as outbound connections. The hardcoded URLs point to AWS and CIS documentation resources.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The public CIDR appears only as a security misconfiguration target. The URLs are user-facing references, not programmatic callbacks or exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: Security-Domain Keywords Trigger Malware Heuristics
low_findings.description: Static C2 and weak-cryptography alerts correspond to terms such as EC2, encryption checks, open network access, and security service names. No command-and-control behavior, cryptographic downgrading, or malicious protocol use was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged words occur inside AWS security assessment examples. Semantic review found no hidden payload, listener, beacon, or weak cryptographic implementation.

low_findings.title: Device File Redirection Suppresses Expected AWS Errors
low_findings.description: The /dev/null redirections are used to suppress expected AWS CLI errors when checking bucket encryption and KMS key rotation. This is a normal shell pattern and not evidence of hiding malicious output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The redirections are adjacent to AWS read operations and error handling checks. There is no filesystem write, persistence, or data hiding behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copyable Shell Blocks With Live Cloud Queries
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains copyable bash blocks that run live AWS API queries through the local shell. This pattern is acceptable for a security-audit skill, but users should review commands before execution and avoid privileged production credentials when possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell blocks are executable and interact with AWS services. Their content is audit-focused and read-only, so human review should focus on operational authorization.

content.value_statement: AWS accounts can accumulate IAM, network, logging, and encryption gaps that are hard to review manually. This skill provides focused AWS CLI and boto3 checks that help Claude, Codex, and Claude Code guide a structured security audit.
actual_capabilities: Guides checks for IAM MFA gaps, root access keys, stale access keys, and broad administrator policies.
Provides AWS CLI examples for open security groups, public S3 buckets, and VPC Flow Logs coverage.
Covers encryption checks for EBS volumes, RDS instances, S3 buckets, and KMS key rotation.
Includes a copyable bash report template for summarizing common AWS security findings.
Maps findings to CIS AWS Foundations, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA control areas.
limitations: Requires correctly configured AWS credentials and permissions before commands can run.
Does not replace AWS Security Hub, GuardDuty, Config, or a full compliance assessment.
Examples may need region, account, and service-specific adjustments.
Generated reports can contain sensitive cloud inventory and should be handled carefully.

use_cases.title: Pre-Audit Cloud Review
use_cases.description: Run structured checks before a formal compliance or customer security review.

use_cases.title: IAM Hygiene Assessment
use_cases.description: Identify missing MFA, old access keys, root account risks, and broad permissions.

use_cases.title: Security Remediation Planning
use_cases.description: Turn audit output into prioritized fixes for encryption, logging, and network exposure.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of one AWS account.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-security-audit to help me review my AWS account. Start with IAM, public network exposure, encryption, and logging checks.

prompt_templates.title: Review IAM Risks
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-security-audit to design an IAM review plan for MFA, root access keys, old access keys, and administrator policies.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Compliance Summary
prompt_templates.scenario: Map findings to common control families.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-security-audit to organize AWS findings into CIS AWS Foundations, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA control areas.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Remediation Roadmap
prompt_templates.prompt: Use aws-security-audit to prioritize these AWS security findings by risk, ownership, remediation effort, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Check my AWS account for common security gaps.
output_examples.output: A categorized audit plan covering IAM, network exposure, data protection, logging, and monitoring checks.

output_examples.input: Summarize these AWS audit results for leadership.
output_examples.output: A concise risk summary with critical fixes, high-priority items, and recommended next steps.

output_examples.input: Map my findings to compliance controls.
output_examples.output: A control-oriented summary linking AWS findings to CIS, PCI-DSS, and HIPAA areas.

best_practices: Run commands with read-only, least-privilege AWS credentials whenever possible.
Review generated commands before execution and confirm the target account and region.
Store audit results in a protected location because they can expose sensitive inventory.
anti_patterns: Do not run audit commands in accounts where you lack explicit authorization.
Do not publish raw audit output in tickets, chats, or public repositories.
Do not treat the examples as a complete compliance attestation.

faq.question: Does this skill make changes to AWS resources?
faq.answer: The documented examples are focused on read-only assessment. Users should still review each command before running it.

faq.question: What permissions are needed?
faq.answer: Use read-only permissions for IAM, EC2, S3, RDS, KMS, CloudTrail, CloudWatch, AWS Config, and related services.

faq.question: Can it replace AWS Security Hub?
faq.answer: No. It helps structure manual checks and reports, but managed AWS security services provide broader continuous monitoring.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code run the commands automatically?
faq.answer: Only if the tool environment has shell access, AWS CLI configuration, and user approval for the command execution.

faq.question: Are audit results sensitive?
faq.answer: Yes. Results can reveal identities, resource names, network exposure, and missing controls.

faq.question: Is this suitable for compliance evidence?
faq.answer: It can support preparation and internal review, but final compliance evidence should follow your audit program requirements.
